fidelity.reason: The planner, first-stage and recourse decisions, timing, objective, information, and installed-capacity restriction are preserved.
evidence_spans: a capacity planner chooses first-stage capacity and a recourse policy
recourse actions occur only after demand is observed and must respect installed capacity

managerial_framing.reason: It clearly establishes the costly advance-capacity versus later shortage-and-recourse trade-off and its decision pathway.
evidence_spans: More advance capacity is expensive but reduces shortage and emergency recourse exposure.

scholarly_positioning.reason: The stated contribution rests only on the topic not having been studied in this context, which is generic context novelty rather than a mechanism-based contribution.
evidence_spans: the same topic has not previously been studied in this particular context

evidence_discipline.reason: The claim is appropriately limited to the modeled distribution and recourse set rather than forecasting certainty.
evidence_spans: The findings depend on the modeled distribution and recourse set rather than perfect demand foresight.

prose_clarity.reason: The model setting and its scope are conveyed economically and in a logical sequence.
evidence_spans: First-stage capacity changes the feasible and costly adjustments available after demand realization.

overall_reason: The model setting is faithful, decision-relevant, and carefully bounded; only the optional contribution statement relies on weak context novelty.
